title: Anion Detection by Electro-Insertion intoN,N,N′, N′-Tetrahexyl-Phenylenediamine (THPD) Microdroplets Studied by Voltammetry, EQCM, and SEM Techniques
abstract:
Anion Detection by Electro-Insertion into N, N, N0, N0-Tetrahexyl-
Phenylenediamine (THPD) Microdroplets Studied by Voltammetry,
EQCM, and SEM Techniques
Frank Marken,*þ Richard G. Compton,þ Christiaan H. Goeting,þ John S. Foord,þ Steven D. Bull,þþ and Stephen G. Daviesþþ
þ The Physical and Theoretical Chemistry Laboratory, Oxford University, Oxford OX1 3QZ, UK
þþDyson Perrins Laboratory, Oxford University, South Parks Road, Oxford OX1 3QY, UK
Received: May 22, 1998
Final version: June 29, 1998
Abstract
The electrochemical oxidation and re-reduction of N0,N0,N,N-tetrahexylphenylene diamine (THPD) deposited in form of microdroplets on a basal
plane pyrolytic graphite or gold electrode is shown to be a chemically reversible process in the presence of aqueous electrolyte media containing
NO3
¹, SCN¹, ClO4
¹, or PF6
¹. Sharp voltammetric responses with a mid point potential, Emid, characteristic of the type of anion and its concentration
are observed. The oxidation product, an ionic liquid, undergoes rapid ion exchange when the anion of the aqueous electrolyte is exchanged with
anions of lower Emid replacing anions of higher Emid. Although the effect of the supporting electrolyte cation in the case of the alkali metals Kþ,
Naþ, and Liþ is not signiﬁcant, a considerable change in the voltammetric peak shape and Emid occurs in the presence of protons. This effect,
attributed to the protonation of THPD, is also sensitive to the type of anion present with anions of lower Emid causing more facile protonation. After
protonation of THPD, oxidation and re-reduction can be shown to be associated with Hþ expulsion and uptake. Deposited onto the rough surface of
a gold coated planoconvex quartz crystal oscillator the [THPDþClO4
¹]oil deposit can be observed in form of micron-sized droplets in SEM images.
A strong frequency response of the crystal oscillator in an electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance experiment associated with the oxidation
and re-reduction of THPD can be detected but is not related to changes in mass. Rather, this frequency response may be attributed to changes in the
viscosity and/or coverage of the oily deposit.
Keywords: Ionic liquid, Oil, Insertion, Voltammetry, Ion exchange, Anion detection
1. Introduction
The accumulation or preconcentration [1, 2] of neutral, anionic,
or cationic materials at an electrode surface often is a prerequisite
for a very low limit of detection and therefore for achieving high
sensitivity in voltammetric analysis. Most notably ‘stripping’
techniques [3] based on mercury drop or ﬁlm electrodes have
been employed in a wide range of applications not only for the
detection of metal cations but also for neutral and anionic species
which form an electroactive adsorbed layer on the surface of the
mercury electrode [4].
The insertion of many types of metal cation from an aqueous
electrolyte solution into liquid mercury usually proceeds as a
chemically and electrochemically reversible process up to a
concentration at which the amalgam solidiﬁes [5]. The potential
at which the redox-induced insertion process occurs is determined
by a Gibbs free energy term composed of contributions from the
aqueous phase, the metal amalgam, and the redox process [6]. By
changing the composition of the aqueous phase the electrochemical
responses for the detection of metal cations may be varied or
separated within a limited range. However, apart from ‘third
element effects’ [6] there is no option to vary the properties of the
amalgam to select a speciﬁc cation.
Preconcentration techniques employing electrodes modiﬁed with
materials such as organic ligands or zeolites with afﬁnity for a
certain type of ion have been suggested [7, 8]. Recent work on
redox processes at microcrystalline materials attached to electrode
surfaces [9] has shown that electrochemically induced ion insertion
processes are surprisingly common for a wide range of materials
including typical
electrically
insulating compounds such as
decamethylferrocene or indigo [10]. An elegant procedure for the
detection of ammonium cations has recently been proposed based
on the reactive insertion into solid Prussian Blue [11]. These ion
insertion processes usually balance the charge for the material being
oxidized or reduced and are governed by the type of ion, the type of
electroactive material, the chemical reversibility of the process, the
structure of the solid, and possibly other factors such as the charge
transport, the crystal face and size as well as the composition of the
aqueous electrolyte.
In order to overcome the complex effects due to the crystal
structure and morphology in a recent publication the use of an
organic electrochemically active compound, N0; N0; N; N-tetrahexyl-
phenylene diamine (THPD, see Scheme 1), designed to be an
extremely insoluble and oily material by substitution with long
alkyl chains, has been studied [13]. Electrochemical processes
accompanied by anion insertion were observed when THPD was
deposited on a basal plane pyrolytic graphite electrode surface in
form of microdroplets and immersed in aqueous electrolyte
solution. Although well-deﬁned and surprisingly sharp voltam-
metric responses were observed for the reversible insertion of
perchlorate anions an exploratory study of the characteristics for
other types of anions revealed that the overall electrochemical
process could be very complex. In this report the chemical
processes accompanying the ion insertion process are studied in
821
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 12
q WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
1040-0397/98/1209-0821 $ 17.50þ.50/0
Scheme 1. N0; N0; N; N-Tetrahexylphenylene diamine.
 more detail and factors important in the development of a new
methodology for the preconcentration and detection of anions are
discussed.
2. Experimental
2.1. Reagents
The electrochemical experiments were performed using high
purity water of resistivity not less than 18 MQ cm (Elgastat, High
Wycombe, Bucks). For the deposition procedure acetonitrile (dried
and distilled, Fisons) and N0; N0; N; N-tetrahexylphenylene diamine
(THPD) prepared following a literature procedure [13] were used.
Other reagents used for the preparation of electrolyte solutions such
as HNO3, HClO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, KPF6, KCl, KSCN, KNO3,
LiClO4, NaClO4, and K2SO4 were obtained from BDH and Aldrich
in analytical or the purest commercially available grade. Solutions
were purged with argon (Pureshield, BOC Gases) for at least 10 min
prior to experiments. All experiments were conducted at 20 6 2 8C.
2.2. Instrumentation
Electrochemical
data
were
recorded
using
an
Autolab
PGSTAT20 system (Eco Chemie, Netherlands). The working
electrode used was a 4.9 mm diameter basal plane pyrolytic
graphite disk electrode (‘Pyrocarbon’, Le Carbone Ltd., UK), and
the reference electrode was a saturated calomel electrode (SCE).
Electrochemical Quartz Crystal Microbalance (EQCM) measure-
ments were carried out using an Autolab PGSTAT20 system
combined with a frequency counter, Fluke PM6680B (Fluke, The
Netherlands). The 10 MHz oscillator Model 230 and the AT-cut gold
coated planoconvex quartz crystals were purchased from the
Technical Department, Instytut Chemii Fizycznej Polskiej Akademii
Nauk (Poland). The working electrode used was a 5 mm diameter
gold disk on one side of the quartz crystal exposed to the solution.
The resonant frequency of the quartz crystal electrode was monitored
simultaneously with the detection of the electrochemical responses.
The sensitivity of the balance, typically 0.5 ng Hz¹1, was calibrated
by employing the Ag deposition process from an aqueous solution of
ca. 0.5 mM AgNO3 and 0.1 M KNO3 [14]. For the deposition of
THPD onto electrode surfaces a solution of 5.2 mg THPD in 100 mL
acetonitrile (116 mM) was transferred and evaporated in air.
3. Results and Discussion
3.1. Voltammetric Detection of Anion Insertion and
Exchange Processes
Redox induced ion exchange processes may be observed for a
wide range of electro-active materials mechanically attached to or
deposited on an electrode surface and immersed in an aqueous
electrolyte solution [9]. For sufﬁciently small particle or droplet
sizes even materials of extremely low electrical conductivity
undergo redox processes and ion uptake/expulsion is required in
order to maintain the electro-neutrality. It has recently been shown
that the organic liquid N0; N0; N; N-tetrahexylphenylene diamine
(THPD) deposited onto a basal plane pyrolytic graphite electrode
surface in form of microdroplets may undergo reversible anion
insertion processes upon oxidation and re-reduction [13]. By optical
microscopy it was demonstrated that this redox process of
microdroplets of THPD attached to a transparent electrode and
immersed in an aqueous 0.1 M NaClO4 solution is associated with a
color change from yellow to dark blue and that for sufﬁciently small
droplets an exhaustive bulk conversion occurs. Depending on the
type of anion the oxidation is chemically reversible or coupled to
follow-up reactions. For the case of a perchlorate containing
aqueous electrolyte solution the ﬁrst oxidation process has been
interpreted in terms of a reversible one-electron–one-anion process
(Eq. 1).
THPD þ ClO¹
4 O ½THPDþClO¹
4 ÿoil þ e¹

of 1 mg THPD deposited on a 4.9 mm diameter basal plane pyrolytic
graphite electrode and immersed in 0.1 M aqueous solutions
containing ClO¹
4 and NO¹
3 (Fig. 1A,B) are shown. In both cases
chemically fully reversible and well-deﬁned responses are observed
and other types of anions such as PF ¹
6 and SCN ¹ were found to
result in similarly shaped reversible voltammetric responses. The
midpoint potentials, Emid, deﬁned here as the potential half way
between the observed peak potentials for oxidation and reduction,
were
determined
as
Emid
(PF¹
6 ) ¼ 0.04 V
(vs.
SCE),
Emid
(ClO¹
4 ) ¼ 0.11 V (vs. SCE), Emid (SCN¹) ¼ 0.18 V (vs. SCE) and
Emid (NO¹
3 ) ¼ 0.30 V (vs. SCE). The peak shape and width for the
reversible anion uptake and expulsion process for these anions
corresponds to that described for the process involving perchlorate
uptake and expulsion [13]. In particular, very sharp ‘spike-like’
responses with peak width at half height less than 20 mV are
observed for 1 mg THPD deposited on a basal plane pyrolytic

3.1. Voltammetric Detection of Anion Insertion and
Redox induced ion exchange processes may be observed for a
wide range of electro-active materials mechanically attached to or
deposited on an electrode surface and immersed in an aqueous
electrolyte solution [9]. For sufﬁciently small particle or droplet
sizes even materials of extremely low electrical conductivity
undergo redox processes and ion uptake/expulsion is required in
order to maintain the electro-neutrality. It has recently been shown
that the organic liquid N0; N0; N; N-tetrahexylphenylene diamine
(THPD) deposited onto a basal plane pyrolytic graphite electrode
surface in form of microdroplets may undergo reversible anion
insertion processes upon oxidation and re-reduction [13]. By optical
microscopy it was demonstrated that this redox process of
microdroplets of THPD attached to a transparent electrode and
immersed in an aqueous 0.1 M NaClO4 solution is associated with a
color change from yellow to dark blue and that for sufﬁciently small
droplets an exhaustive bulk conversion occurs. Depending on the
type of anion the oxidation is chemically reversible or coupled to
follow-up reactions. For the case of a perchlorate containing
aqueous electrolyte solution the ﬁrst oxidation process has been
interpreted in terms of a reversible one-electron–one-anion process
In Figure 1 cyclic voltammograms for the oxidation and reduction
of 1 mg THPD deposited on a 4.9 mm diameter basal plane pyrolytic
graphite electrode and immersed in 0.1 M aqueous solutions
3 (Fig. 1A,B) are shown. In both cases
chemically fully reversible and well-deﬁned responses are observed
and other types of anions such as PF ¹
result in similarly shaped reversible voltammetric responses. The
midpoint potentials, Emid, deﬁned here as the potential half way
between the observed peak potentials for oxidation and reduction,
4 ) ¼ 0.11 V (vs. SCE), Emid (SCN¹) ¼ 0.18 V (vs. SCE) and
3 ) ¼ 0.30 V (vs. SCE). The peak shape and width for the
reversible anion uptake and expulsion process for these anions
corresponds to that described for the process involving perchlorate
uptake and expulsion [13]. In particular, very sharp ‘spike-like’
responses with peak width at half height less than 20 mV are
observed for 1 mg THPD deposited on a basal plane pyrolytic
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 12
Fig. 1. Cyclic voltammograms for the oxidation and re-reduction of 1 mg
THPD deposited onto a 4.9 mm diameter basal plane pyrolytic graphite
electrode and immersed in A) aqueous 0.1 M NaClO4 solution, B) 0.1 M
KNO3 solution, C) ﬁrst 0.1 M NaClO4 and on the reverse scan in 0.1 M
KNO3, and D) ﬁrst in 0.1 M KNO3 and on the reverse scan in 0.1 M NaClO4.
graphite electrode immersed in 0.1 M electrolyte containing
solution and at a sufﬁciently slow scan rate of less than
10 mV s¹1. For this type of response the peak current is increasing
approximately linear with scan rate in agreement with an essentially
complete electrolysis of a thin layer of deposited material. The
characteristic shape of these voltammetric responses is believed to
arise from strong interaction in the ionic liquid phase which induces
non-Nernstian behavior by favoring the formation of product in the
vicinity of already existing product. Optical microscopy [13]
conﬁrmed the presence of two phases, the oily starting material and
the ionic liquid, coexisting apparently without mixing, at least on a
minutes time scale. A theoretical description of the characteristics
of voltammetric responses distorted by interaction between the
redox centres has been suggested by Laviron [15].
The characteristic shift in midpoint potential observed for the
reversible ion-insertion processes for the different anions may be
attributed to the Gibbs free energy term responsible for the transfer of
the anion form the aqueous into the organic phase. Qualitatively, a
more hydrophobic anion, e.g., PF¹
6 , undergoes uptake into the organic
phase at a more negative potential. However, speciﬁc interaction
between the anion and the THPDþ cation in the organic phase will, at
least in principle, contribute and could be important in determining the
potential at which the redox process occurs. The voltammetric
responses shown in Figure 1 are similarly affected by changes in the
concentration of the anion as has been demonstrated recently for the
case of perchlorate [13]. The corresponding shift of the midpoint
potential ofapproximately 60mVper decadic change inconcentration
is in agreement with the process described by Equation 1.
Under the experimental conditions used in this study the ion
uptake is fast and from the shift in potential between the perchlorate
and the nitrate insertion processes it can be concluded that the
4 is thermodynamically preferable. In fact, ion
exchange may occur very rapidly when the oxidized material
3 anions is transferred into a solution containing
4 . In Figure 1C a potential sweep for the oxidation of THPD in
4 is shown. The potential was held at E ¼ 0.4 V (vs.
SCE) and the supporting electrolyte changed to 0.1 M NO ¹
the reversed potential scan was recorded. The observed reduction
response with a peak at Ep ¼ 0.19 V (vs. SCE) is associated with the
anions into the nitrate containing supporting
electrolyte. The shape and position of this ‘anion-stripping’ response
is governed by the surface concentration of the perchlorate in the
vicinity of the electrode surface or, more accurately, in the vicinity of
the microdroplet–aqueous solution interface. In Figure 1D the
complementary experiment with a negative potential sweep in the
3 after oxidation in 0.1 M ClO¹
containing electrolyte solution and exchanging the electrolyte
solution at a potential of E ¼ 0.4 V (vs. SCE) is shown. After
changing the supporting electrolyte and reversing the potential sweep
direction immediately the characteristic response for the ClO¹
expulsion process is detected! Thus, in a rapid equilibration process
(within seconds) nitrate anions have been exchanged for perchlorate
anions before the electrochemical reduction occurred (Eq. 2).
This kind of rapid ion exchange reaction is not surprising and has
recently also been observed for processes associated with processes
at solid electroactive materials [16].
3.2. The Effect of the Proton Concentration on
The redox chemistry of THPD microdroplets may be complicated
by i) the second oxidation step which yields a chemically less inert
THPD2þ dication [17], ii) coinsertion of neutral or charged species
leading to the uptake of a ‘cluster-anion’ rather than only the simple
anionic species, iii) the possible protonation of THPD which occurs
readily at the electron-rich nitrogen atom, or combinations of these
The protonation process can be demonstrated to be the cause for
considerable changes in the voltammetric responses associated with
anion uptake. In Figure 2 the oxidation of 0.5 mg THPD deposited
on a 4.9 mm diameter basal plane pyrolytic graphite electrode and
immersed in a 0.1 M KNO3 solution is shown. Initially, no acid was
present and then the pH was adjusted by gradually adding HNO3. It
can be observed that at a proton concentration of approximately
pH 4 a considerable change in wave shape occurs and the addition
of acid then causes the broad voltammetric response with a peak
width at half height of ca. 100 mV to shift towards more positive
potentials. The shift in potential is a characteristic indication for the
expulsion of protons upon oxidation and a plot of the shift in mid
point potential versus pH for the four anions PF ¹
4 is shown in Figure 3. The observed trends are very
similar, although, there are characteristic details associated with the
nature of the anions. The most hydrophobic anion, PF ¹
uptake into the THPD droplet at neutral pH most readily with a

THPD deposited onto a 4.9 mm diameter basal plane pyrolytic graphite
electrode and immersed in aqueous 0.1 M KNO3 solution. The pH of the
solution was adjusted by addition of aqueous HNO3. Scan rate 10 mV s¹1.

oxidation and re-reduction of 0.5 mg THPD deposited onto a 4.9 mm diameter
basal plane pyrolytic graphite electrode immersed in aqueous 0.1 M KNO3
(pH adjusted with HNO3), 0.1 M KSCN (pH adjusted with HNO3), 0.1 M
NaClO4 (pH adjusted with HClO4), 0.1 M KPF6 (pH adjusted with HNO3).
midpoint potential of Emid(PF ¹
6 ) ¼ 0.04 V (vs. SCE). Accordingly,
the change of the voltammetric characteristics due to the presence
of acid, which can be determined by an extrapolation method
6 already at a proton concentration of ca.
3 this transition appears to occur
at pH 5.2, pH 5.0 and pH 4.0, respectively. Data points for proton
concentrations with pH > 4 are not included in Figure 3 because a
buffer system would be necessary to maintain the equilibrium.
The pKA for N; N; N0; N0-tetramethyl- and N; N; N0; N0,-tetra-
ethyl-phenylenediamine (TMPD and TEPD) in aqueous hydro-
chloric acid have been reported [18a] as pKA1,TMPD ¼ 6.5,
THPD only hypothetical values based on the correlation procedure
by Advanced Chemical Development Inc. [18b] for zero ionic
strength and 25 8C are available with pKA1 ¼ 7.5 6 0.5 and
pKA2 ¼ 3.9 6 0.5. These values, although valid only for the
protonation of a species dissolved in an aqueous solution, are in
ﬁrst approximation consistent with the interpretation of the
observed process as protonation of THPD. At more acidic pH
values the process is coupled reversibly to the concentration of acid
in the aqueous phase which means that oxidation of THPD is
accompanied by the expulsion of protons and the reduced form
of THPD is in a protonated equilibrium state. As indicated in
the plot (Fig. 3) over a limited range, from pH 2 to 4, the shift in
mid point potential with proton concentration follows approxi-
mately a 60 mV per pH unit dependence and therefore suggests the
following mechanism to be operative in sufﬁciently acidic solution
½THPDHþX¹ÿoil O ½THPDþX¹ÿoil þ e¹ þ Hþ
This process is based on the insertion of protons from the aqueous
solution phase in order to maintain charge neutrality upon reduction
of the oil phase. The anions must remain ‘resident’ in the oil phase.
Attempts to use THPD deposited on a basal plane pyrolytic graphite
electrode surface for the insertion of Liþ in aqueous 0.1 M LiClO4
or Naþ in 0.1 M NaClO4 solution were not successful. In both cases
the simple anion insertion occurs and the cation is not signiﬁcantly
affecting the electrochemical process. The selectivity of the process
towards protons may be explained by the facile protonation of the
Many simple singly charged anions, such as PF¹
4 , undergo reversible insertion into THPD droplets and are
detectable as well-deﬁned voltammetric responses. However, other
types of anions such as Cl¹ and I¹ [13] give more complicated
voltammetric responses or they trigger irreversible chemical
changes as in the case of the hydroxide anion [13]. Interestingly
double charged anions such as sulfate and HPO2¹
reversible insertion and a protonated mono-anion such as H2PO¹
also does not give a detectable voltammetric response in the
potential region in which NO ¹
3 is detected. In these cases there is
always an anodic voltammetric response at more positive potentials
detectable but this is chemically irreversible and the analysis of the
process will require more detailed work employing techniques
which yield complementary information about the chemical
composition of the product. These observations appear to suggest
that, in part, the chemistry of THPD in the oil phase, involving
protonation, disproportionation, and possibly other chemical steps,
is responsible for the nature and the selectivity towards ion insertion
for the electrochemically driven redox processes.
3.3. EQCM Detection of Anion Insertion Processes
The voltammetric detection of redox processes in small oil
droplets of THPD has been shown to occur on basal plane pyrolytic
graphite as well as on other materials such as gold and indium tin
oxide (ITO) coated glass [13]. However, the characteristics of the
voltammetric response are affected by the droplet size and
distribution, and therefore are to some extent sensitive to the
electrode material and morphology. Electrochemical quartz crystal
microbalance (EQCM) experiments [19, 20], which proved a
powerful tool in the investigation of redox processes of solid
particles [16], are commonly performed with gold coated quartz
crystal oscillators. Depending on the type of polishing procedure
and treatment of the quartz surface before gold coating a change
in deposition pattern may be expected. SEM images of a gold
coated planoconvex quartz crystal used in this study are shown in
Figure 4. The surface roughness on a micron-scale originates from
the underlying quartz substrate. The dark areas which can be seen in
the ‘valleys’ are due to a deposit of [THPDþClO ¹
to absorb the electron beam very efﬁciently. From the distribution
of the dark material it can be seen that the surface morphology of
the unpolished quartz helps forming an evenly dispersed coating of
what appears to be microdroplets. This surface morphology is
ideally suited for the electrochemical conversion of THPD because
it prevents the formation of larger droplet-aggregates.

Fig. 4. Scanning electron microscopy images of [THPDþClO¹
from 1 mg THPD deposited onto a 5 mm diameter gold coated planoconvex
quartz crystal oscillator and oxidized electrochemically in aqueous 0.1 M
Fig. 5. Cyclic voltammograms for the oxidation and re-reduction of 0.5 mg
THPD deposited onto a 5 mm diameter gold coated quartz crystal oscillator
immersed in aqueous 0.1 M NaClO4 solution. Scan rates 1, 2, 5, 10 mV s¹1.
The voltammetric responses observed for the oxidation and
reduction of the THPD deposit on the gold coated quartz (Fig. 5)
immersed in aqueous 0.1 M NaClO4 are well deﬁned and
correspond to those observed on basal plane pyrolytic graphite.
However, in Figure 5 it can be seen that at conventional scan rates
the peak width is substantially wider and this change may be
attributed to the possibly larger average size of the droplets. At a
low scan rate of 1 mV s¹1 for the oxidation and re-reduction of
0.5 mg THPD deposited on the 5 mm diameter gold coated quartz
crystal and immersed in 0.1 M NaClO4 solution again narrow peak
widths in the order of 20 mV are observed.
The EQCM response for switching the redox-state of the THPD
oil droplets may be monitored simultaneously with the current
response (Fig. 6). After deposition of the THPD and immersion of
the electrode in the aqueous electrolyte a considerable drift of the
resonance frequency is observed which stabilizes after cycling the
potential between oxidation and reduction of THPD about 10 times.
Then the change in resonance frequency follows the current
response in the way expected for the electrochemical reaction
(Fig. 6). However, in this case the data are considerably different
compared to results obtained with solids mechanically attached to
gold coated quartz crystals [16]. In Figure 6 a voltammogram for
the oxidation of 0.2 mg THPD in 0.1 M NaClO4 is shown with a
charge of 34 mC corresponding to an electrochemical conversion of
80 % of the material. The overall change in the resonance frequency
of the quartz crystal, 110 Hz, which is due to the uneven distribution
on the quartz oscillator and the nonrigid nature of the material is not
expected to be in good agreement with the theoretically expected
value of 70 Hz, is in the right order of magnitude to account for a
weight change. However, increasing the amount of deposited
THPD results in a considerably higher mismatch between expected
to observed change in frequency with the latter being too large.
Further, experiments conducted by exchanging the electrolyte
solution to other ions such as iodide and chloride generally resulted
in similar frequency changes without any signiﬁcant correlation to
the weight of the inserted anion. Both of the above ﬁndings suggest
that the viscosity of the oily material is not sufﬁciently high to
couple efﬁciently to the 10 MHz vibration of the quartz crystal.
presumably due to viscosity changes and/or surface tension
effects rather than a mass change. Nevertheless, the EQCM is
sensitive to the redox process and useful qualitative information
related to the change, e.g., of viscosity or surface tension may be
obtained. The results further conﬁrm that the THPD droplets after
oxidation and uptake of an anion from the aqueous phase remain in
An interesting feature of the voltammetry of microdroplets of
THPD deposited on suitable electrode surfaces and immersed in
aqueous solution is the reversible uptake of anions from the solution
phase upon oxidation. For a range of anions from PF¹
similar voltammetric characteristics with a distinct shift in
reversible potential were detected. Although the chemistry of
THPD clearly limits the possible range of reversible anion insertion
processes due to protonation and due to a second potentially
irreversible redox step there appears to be a new methodology
which, when applied to a system with a more speciﬁc oil–ion
interaction, could result in an efﬁcient and simple analytical tool for
the detection of ionic species in aqueous media. In order to achieve
the quantitative determination of the concentration of a particular
ion by indirect amperometric or gravimetric detection with oil
microdroplets a more speciﬁc oil-ion interaction is necessary to
avoid the shift of the low concentration analyte signal into the
excess supporting electrolyte signal or, better, a microelectrode
array system may be used without excess electrolyte. Further work
on this and the details of the electrochemical and coupled chemical
processes occurring in THPD microdroplets and other related
